revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:従四位]][[ko:종4위]][[zh:從四位]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:従四位]][[ko:종4위]][[zh:從四位]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:従四位]][[ko:종4위]][[zh:從四位]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:従四位]][[ko:종4위]][[zh:從四位]]
{{Cite notice|date=May 21, 2013}} '''Jushi''' (従四位) is one of the {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japanese|1=Japan}} [[Rank system|court Ranks]] and [[Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=Shinkai|divine Ranks]]. It is situated below {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank|ja|正四位|1=Senior Fourth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fourth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fourth Rank|qq=}} and above {{ill|Senior Fifth Rank|ja|正五位|1=Senior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fifth Rank|qq=}}. When conferred posthumously, it is called ''Zō Jushi''.<ref>https://www.wakapoetry.net/background/society/court-offices-titles-and-Ranks/court-Ranks/</ref>
== Explanation == Under the [[Ritsuryō system]], the Rank of Jushi was divided into upper (Jushi-no-jō) and lower (Jushi-no-ge) Ranks. It corresponded to [[Kun’ō]] in [[Kun’ei]] and equivalent administrative positions such as [[Daijō-kan]]’s {{ill|Benkan|en|Benkan|qid=Q124412016|lt=Sa-Udaiben|1=Benkan}}, senior officials (''Taifu'') in {{ill|Shiki|en|Shiki|qid=Q258572|lt=Shiki|1=Shiki}} (律令制), middle captains in the [[Konoe Fuchū|Imperial Guards]], [[Kebiishi Betto]], [[:en:Kurōdodokoro|Kurōdō heads]], and ''Taifu'' of [[Kyōshiki]], which managed the {{ill|Imperial Court|en|Imperial Court|qid=Q6006523|lt=Imperial Court|1=Imperial Court}}’s administration. This Rank was also eligible for {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|qid=Q1133663|lt=Sangi (Japan)|1=Sangi (Japan)}} appointments, enabling inclusion among the {{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|qid=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō|1=Kugyō}} (nobility).
For individuals holding lower Jushi (Jushi-no-ge) or higher Ranks but not yet Kugyō, the respectful address attached [[Asomi]] to the family name and given name, following the pattern ''surname-given name-Asomi,'' such as "Hosokawa Ukyō Daibu Jushi-no-ge Minamoto Katsumoto Asomi."
During the [[Ritsuryō system]], where Ranks below Goshi and Jushi were considered lower nobility and those above Sangi were considered upper nobility, individuals could ascend to Goshi through merit in civil and military services. However, promotion to Jushi was limited to specific aristocratic clans, with rare exceptions for extraordinary achievements. The [[Shinki era]] decree of 728 (''Shinki 5'') effectively barred external clans from reaching Jushi or higher Ranks<ref>Sagimori Hiroyuki, "The Internal and External Hierarchies of the Nobility," in ''The Politics of the Emperor and Nobility in Ancient Japan'' (Hanawa Shobo, 2018).</ref>.
Later, members of [[:en:Fujiwara Clan|Fujiwara Clan]]’s peripheral branches and mid-tier non-Fujiwara aristocrats often held the Rank. During the era of the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|qid=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji|1=Seiwa Genji}} and [[Kanmu Heishi]] families, military clan leaders typically began with Goshi-no-ge and considered Shoshi the highest attainable Rank for their lineage.
Following the establishment of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|qid=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, the [[Hōjō Clan]], seizing the ''shikken'' position and eliminating political rivals, saw its main line (''Tōkuso'') achieve the upper Jushi Rank (Jushi-no-jō). Other prominent {{ill|Gokenin|en|Gokenin|qid=Q63765|lt=Gokenin|1=Gokenin}} also rose to Jushi Ranks. By the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, powerful [[shugo daimyo]] were included in the Rank, especially among families like the [[Ashikaga Clan]]’s vassals and other prestigious shugo lineages.
During the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, a shift occurred where [[:en:retainers|retainers]] of families like the [[Miyoshi Clan]], initially Ranked as deputy {{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|qid=Q1141023|lt=Shugo|1=Shugo}}, gained Jushi titles. For instance, {{ill|Matsunaga Hisahide|en|Matsunaga Hisahide|qid=Q1143038|lt=Matsunaga Hisahide|1=Matsunaga Hisahide}}, a Miyoshi retainer, received Jushi-no-ge, indicating a merit-based appointment system.
With {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}'s rise to power after {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|qid=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}}'s demise, heavyweights like {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} and {{ill|Maeda Toshiie|en|Maeda Toshiie|qid=Q1196817|lt=Maeda Toshiie|1=Maeda Toshiie}} attained higher court Ranks, leaving Jushi for their heirs and affiliates.
Under the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]], Jushi was assigned to senior officials, {{ill|Kōke|en|Kōke|qid=Q11669183|lt=Kōke|1=Kōke}}, and select [[:en:Fudai daimyo|Fudai daimyo]] or [[:en:tozama daimyo|tozama daimyo]] ruling territories over 100,000 koku. {{See|List of Daimyo Families Promoted Above Fourth Rank}}.
In 1728, during {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|en|Tokugawa Yoshimune|qid=Q319664|lt=Tokugawa Yoshimune|1=Tokugawa Yoshimune}}'s reign, a {{ill|Vietnam|en|Vietnam|qid=Q881|lt=Vietnam|1=Vietnam}}ese elephant presented to the shogunate was reportedly granted Jushi as "Jushi Kōnan Hakuzō" to permit entry to the [[:en:Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]]. However, contemporary records lack evidence of this conferment<ref>{{cite journal|title=State Management and Elephant Trade in Pre-modern Vietnam|author=Phan Hai Linh|journal=Annual Report of the Ancient East Eurasian Research Center, Senshu University|year=2018|volume=4|page=51}}</ref>.
In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, Jushi corresponded to baronial Ranks under the [[Kazoku]] system and was equivalent to lieutenant general in the {{ill|Imperial Army|en|Imperial Army|qid=Q1207511|lt=Imperial Army|1=Imperial Army}} and {{ill|Imperial Navy|en|Imperial Navy|qid=Q1195038|lt=Imperial Navy|1=Imperial Navy}}. The [[Order of Rank Regulations]] designated Jushi as an honor bestowed by imperial decree, treated with nobility-like courtesy<ref group="note">Article 4 of the ''Order of Rank Regulations'' stipulates that "Ranks of Jushi or above are conferred by imperial decree, executed through the Minister of the Imperial Household."</ref>.
Today, Jushi is conferred posthumously on individuals recognized for meritorious contributions to public service, academia, arts, or cultural achievements, including government officials, artists, and scholars.
{{Court Ranks of Japan}} * <references />
{{translated page|ja|従四位|version=104618368|comment=Imported full ja history}}
